content: This qEEG narrative report presents a comprehensive analysis of spectral power, coherence, and connectivity patterns. The recording was performed under standard conditions with eyes-closed resting state protocol. Spectral analysis reveals elevated theta power in frontal regions with reduced alpha power posteriorly. Coherence findings indicate reduced interhemispheric coherence in the alpha band. Connectivity summary shows disrupted default mode network connectivity. The clinical impression suggests patterns consistent with attention deficit presentations, though this finding is indicative and should be interpreted in clinical context. Differential considerations include anxiety-related patterns and medication effects. Recommendations include neurofeedback targeting theta/beta ratio with moderate confidence level. Limitations include single-session recording and potential medication confounds. The confidence level for these findings is indicative.

sections.body: Patient is a 34-year-old right-handed individual presenting for quantitative EEG assessment. No history of seizure disorder. Current medications include sertraline 50mg daily. Previous neuroimaging unremarkable. Referred by treating psychiatrist for evaluation of attention and concentration difficulties reported over the past six months. No family history of neurological conditions. Education level is college graduate with reported cognitive baseline above average prior to symptom onset.

Recording performed using 19-channel international 10-20 system montage with linked-ear reference. Impedances maintained below 5 kOhm throughout recording. Eyes-closed resting state condition for 20 minutes. Sampling rate 256 Hz with bandpass filter 0.5-70 Hz. Artifact rejection performed using independent component analysis removing ocular and muscle artifacts. Total artifact-free epochs: 180 seconds. Recording environment was a quiet, dimly lit room with controlled temperature. Patient was alert and cooperative throughout the recording session.

Absolute power analysis reveals elevated theta (4-8 Hz) activity in frontal leads (Fz, F3, F4) exceeding 1.5 standard deviations above normative database values. Alpha (8-12 Hz) power is reduced in posterior regions (Pz, O1, O2) falling 1.2 standard deviations below norms. Beta (13-30 Hz) activity is within normal limits across all sites. Delta (1-4 Hz) power shows mild elevation in central regions. Gamma (30-45 Hz) activity is unremarkable. Peak alpha frequency is 9.8 Hz, within normal range. Theta/beta ratio at Fz is 3.2, elevated relative to age-matched controls (normative mean 2.1). These spectral patterns are consistent with previously reported profiles in attention-related conditions.

Interhemispheric coherence analysis reveals reduced alpha-band coherence between homologous frontal electrode pairs (F3-F4: 0.45 vs normative 0.65). Theta-band coherence is elevated in frontocentral regions suggesting increased slow-wave synchronization. Beta-band coherence values are within normal limits. Short-distance coherence patterns are generally preserved while long-distance coherence shows selective reduction in the alpha range. These patterns suggest altered functional connectivity that may relate to reported attentional difficulties. Phase lag analysis indicates delayed frontal alpha phase relationships compared to normative expectations.

Graph-theoretic analysis of functional connectivity reveals reduced global efficiency in the alpha band network. Default mode network nodes show decreased connectivity strength, particularly between medial prefrontal and posterior cingulate regions as estimated from scalp recordings. The frontoparietal attention network shows altered hub metrics with reduced betweenness centrality at frontal nodes. Small-world network properties are preserved with sigma index of 1.8. These connectivity patterns are consistent with attention-related functional network disruptions reported in the literature. Network modularity analysis reveals intact segregation but reduced integration metrics.

The qEEG findings are suggestive of a pattern commonly associated with attention-related conditions, characterized by elevated frontal theta activity, reduced posterior alpha, and altered interhemispheric coherence. These patterns have been described in peer-reviewed literature as biomarkers associated with attention deficit presentations, though they are not independently diagnostic. The elevated theta/beta ratio at frontal sites is a well-studied metric in this population. Findings should be interpreted in the context of the complete clinical picture including behavioral assessment, clinical history, and psychometric testing. This interpretation carries an indicative confidence level.

Alternative explanations for the observed patterns should be considered. Anxiety disorders can produce similar frontal theta elevations and coherence changes. Medication effects, particularly from SSRI therapy, may influence spectral power distributions and should be factored into interpretation. Sleep deprivation or fatigue could contribute to increased slow-wave activity. Subclinical mood disorder presentations may overlap with the observed connectivity patterns. Age-related normative variations have been accounted for using the reference database. The differential requires integration with clinical assessment and longitudinal follow-up to clarify the primary contributing factors.

Based on the observed qEEG patterns, the following recommendations are offered with indicative confidence: (1) Consider neurofeedback protocol targeting theta suppression and beta enhancement at frontal sites (Fz, Cz), with sessions of 30 minutes, 2-3 times weekly for 20 sessions as an initial course. (2) Repeat qEEG assessment after intervention to evaluate neurophysiological response. (3) Integrate findings with neuropsychological testing results for comprehensive treatment planning. (4) Consider pharmacological consultation if neurofeedback response is insufficient after initial course. (5) Monitor theta/beta ratio as a quantitative outcome measure across treatment sessions.

This report is based on a single recording session which may not capture the full range of the individual's neurophysiological variability. State-dependent factors including caffeine intake, sleep quality, and time of day may influence findings. The normative database used for comparison, while age and gender matched, may not fully account for all demographic and clinical variables. qEEG measures are correlational and should not be interpreted as causal or diagnostic in isolation. Medication effects have not been pharmacologically controlled. Source localization estimates from scalp recordings carry inherent spatial resolution limitations.
